sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 82 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT had no impact again. The player can't move RIGHT because the merged object is blocking.
Let me think about this more carefully. The player is at (24,40)-(26,43), and the merged object is at (24,44)-(31,47). The player is adjacent to the merged object but can't enter it.
The white strip is at (27,40)-(31,43), which is below the player and to the left of the merged object.
Maybe I need to move the white strip into a position where it can merge with the merged object. The white strip is attached to the player, so moving the player moves the white strip.
Let me try moving DOWN to get the player below the merged object, then move RIGHT to get the white strip aligned with the merged object.
Actually, the player is at (24,40)-(26,43), and the merged object is at (24,44)-(31,47). If I move DOWN, the player would move to (25,40)-(27,43), and the white strip would move to (28,40)-(32,43). The white strip would still be to the left of the merged object.
Let me try moving LEFT first, then DOWN, then RIGHT multiple times to get the white strip to the right of the merged object, then move LEFT to merge.
Actually, that doesn't make sense either.
Let me think about what happened earlier when I successfully merged the first two squares. The sequence was:
1. Player at (28,40)-(30,43), white strip at (28,40)-(30,43) (just the player)
2. Pressed SPACE to collect center square (white strip extended to include center square)
3. Moved RIGHT to align with right square
4. Moved DOWN to merge
So the pattern was: collect square → move to target → move in direction that pushes white strip into target.
Currently, I have the white strip with one blue center (from the left square), and the merged object has two blue centers (from center and right squares). I need to merge them.
The white strip is attached to the player, and the player can't move RIGHT because the merged object is blocking. But earlier, when I moved UP, the white strip moved UP with the player, and then I was able to move RIGHT (but it had no impact).
Wait, earlier when I moved UP from (28,40), the player went to (27,40)-(29,43), and the white strip went to (30,40)-(34,43). Then I moved RIGHT, but it had no impact because the player was still blocked by the merged object.
Let me try a different approach. What if I need to move the player to a position where the white strip is ABOVE the merged object, then move DOWN to push the white strip into the merged object?
To get the white strip above the merged object, I need the player to be above the merged object. The merged object is at rows 24-31. If the player is at rows 20-22 (above the merged object), and the white strip is below the player, then the white strip would be at rows 23-27, which overlaps with the merged object at rows 24-27.
Let me try moving UP multiple times to get the player above the merged object, then move RIGHT to align, then move DOWN to merge.
Actually wait, the player is currently at (24,40)-(26,43). The merged object starts at row 24. So the player is already at the same row as the top of the merged object. If I move UP, the player would go to (23,40)-(25,43), which is above the merged object's top row.
Let me try: UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN. This would move the player above the merged object, then move RIGHT to align the white strip with the merged object, then move DOWN to push the white strip into the merged object.
But wait, earlier when I tried UP, RIGHT, the RIGHT had no impact. So the player can't move RIGHT when adjacent to the merged object.
Let me try a different approach. What if I need to move the player to the RIGHT of the merged object, then move LEFT to push the white strip into the merged object?
To get to the right of the merged object, I need to go around it. The merged object is at (24,44)-(31,47). I can go below it by moving DOWN, then RIGHT, then UP.
Let me try: DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT. This would move the player below the merged object, then to the right, then up, then left to push the white strip into the merged object.
But this is getting complicated. Let me first check if the player can move DOWN from the current position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at (24,40)-(26,43), try moving DOWN to go around merged object
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
